questions.explanation: Data science combines statistical thinking, programming, and domain context to derive insights and build data-driven solutions.

options.A: A discipline that uses data, statistics, and computing to extract insights and support decisions
options.B: A method for storing data in relational tables only
options.C: A process for designing computer hardware for analytics
options.D: A technique for encrypting data to prevent access

questions.question: Which statement best defines data science?

questions.explanation: A p-value is computed under the assumption that the null hypothesis is true and measures how extreme the observed data are under that assumption.

options.A: The probability that the null hypothesis is true
options.B: The probability of observing results at least as extreme as the data, assuming the null hypothesis is true
options.C: The probability that the alternative hypothesis is false
options.D: The probability that the sample mean equals the population mean

questions.question: In statistics, what does a p-value represent (in the context of a hypothesis test)?

questions.explanation: Lists preserve order and can be modified (mutable), unlike tuples (immutable) and sets (unordered).
questions.question: In Python, which data structure is an ordered, mutable collection commonly used to store a sequence of items?

questions.explanation: Histograms show how values are distributed across bins, making them a standard choice for a single numeric variable.
questions.question: Which visualization is generally most appropriate for showing the distribution of a single numeric variable?

questions.explanation: A left join keeps all rows from the left DataFrame (orders) and brings in matching customer fields when available.
questions.question: You need to combine two Pandas DataFrames, orders and customers, using customer_id so that all orders are kept even if customer details are missing. Which operation should you use?

questions.explanation: You must aggregate per customer using GROUP BY, then sort by the aggregated SUM and limit to 5.

options.A: SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM purchases ORDER BY revenue DESC LIMIT 5;
options.B: SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM purchases GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY revenue DESC LIMIT 5;
options.C: SELECT TOP 5 customer_id, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM purchases GROUP BY customer_id;
options.D: SELECT customer_id, AVG(amount) AS revenue FROM purchases GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY revenue DESC LIMIT 5;

questions.question: In SQL, which query returns the top 5 customers by total revenue (amount) from a table purchases(customer_id, amount)?

questions.explanation: With severe class imbalance, accuracy can be misleading; precision-recall focuses on performance for the positive class and is often more informative.
questions.question: You are building a binary classifier on an imbalanced dataset (1% positive class). Which evaluation metric is typically most informative for model selection?

questions.explanation: Using information from the future relative to the prediction point leaks target-related data into training, inflating offline performance and breaking production validity.

options.A: Underfitting due to too few features
options.B: Data leakage because the feature uses future information not available at prediction time
options.C: High bias caused by using too many trees
options.D: Class imbalance because churn is rare

questions.question: You are training a model to predict customer churn and you compute a feature: 'number of support tickets in the next 30 days'. Your model performance is excellent but fails in production. What is the most likely issue?

questions.explanation: For time-ordered data, walk-forward validation preserves temporal order and avoids training on future data relative to the validation fold.

options.D: Train-test split stratified by the target label

questions.question: Your cross-validation AUC is 0.92, but the test AUC drops to 0.71. You suspect the model is overfitting and want a validation approach that better reflects future performance on time-ordered data. What should you use?

questions.explanation: Even if protected attributes are removed, correlated variables like ZIP code can encode them indirectly, leading to discriminatory outcomes.

options.A: Differential privacy guarantees will be violated by removing race
options.B: Proxy discrimination, because ZIP code can act as a proxy for protected attributes
options.C: Simpson’s paradox, because aggregated data always reverses correlations
options.D: Overfitting, because fewer features always increase variance

questions.question: A company wants to deploy a model that predicts loan default risk. They plan to exclude protected attributes (e.g., race) but keep ZIP code. Which ethical risk is most relevant?
